title: Studies on the potential of coal fly ash as a heterogeneous catalyst in oxidation of aqueous sodium sulfide solutions with hydrogen peroxide
abstract:
J. Chem. T echnol. Biotechnol. 1997, 70, 231È240
Studies on the Potential of Coal Fly Ash as a
Heterogeneous Catalyst in Oxidation of Aqueous
Sodium Sulfide Solutions with Hydrogen Peroxide
Durjoy Mallick, Swapan Khanra & Swades K. Chaudhuri*
Department of Chemical Engineering, Calcutta University, 92, A.P.C. Road, Calcutta-700 009, India
(Received 7 January 1997; revised version received 9 June 1997; accepted 3 July 1997)
Abstract: The potential of Ñy ash procured form coal-Ðred thermal power plants
was studied as a heterogeneous catalyst in the oxidation of aqueous sodium
sulÐde solutions with hydrogen peroxide in the temperature range of 303È323 K.
The e†ects of various parameters (source of Ñy ash, Ñy ash loading, initial con-
centrations of sodium sulÐde and hydrogen peroxide, electrolyte and deactiva-
tion of catalytic e†ect of Ñy ash) were studied. For an initial sodium sulÐde and
hydrogen
peroxide
concentration
of
26É98 ] 10~2 kmol
m~3
and
24É28 ] 10~2 kmol m~3 respectively, only 4% (w/v) Ñy ash loading intensiÐed
the rate of oxidation by a factor of 4É52 over that without Ñy ash at 303 K. The
deactivation of the catalytic e†ect of Ñy ash was found to be less than 20% even
after six repeated uses. The kinetics of aqueous phase decomposition of hydrogen
peroxide was also studied in the presence of Ñy ash in alkaline medium.
J. Chem. T echnol. Biotechnol. 70, 231È240 (1997)

catalyst

INTRODUCTION
Fly ash is being produced in ever-increasing amounts as
solid waste of coal-Ðred thermal power plants. As the
disposal of this waste has now attained complex dimen-
sions, many research groups have been trying to Ðnd
out its various e†ective utilities. Fly ash contains Ðne
particles of a number of metallic and non-metallic
oxides such as Al2O3 , Fe2O3 , Mn2O3 , TiO2 , SiO2 ,
etc. and/or their complexes such as quartz, mullite,

hematite, ferrite, magnetite, rutile, etc. along with some
unburnt carbon in varying amounts.1,2 Most of the
above metal oxides, metal complexes and/or unburnt
activated carbon are frequently used to catalyze the
decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the course of
oxidative puriÐcation of various hazardous industrial
effluents to less toxic environmentally dischargeable
effluents.3h5 Hence, Ñy ash seems to be an active and
cheap heterogeneous catalyst in
oxidation
H2O2-based
of various industrial wastewaters. On the other hand Ñy
ash, because of its pozzolanic property, is widely used in
the Ðeld of solidiÐcation and/or stabilization of several
toxic sludges and other solid wastes,6h8 hence the dis-
posal of spent Ñy ash may not be a problem. Therefore,
the sequential utilization of Ñy ash as a catalyst in efflu-
ent treatment and as a pozzolan of solid wastes of an
industry may also be an e†ective partial solution for its
acute disposal problem. Although considerable amounts
of work have been reported on the pozzolanic property
of Ñy ash in solidifying and/or stabilizing toxic sludges
and solid wastes, very little attention has been paid to
its catalytic property.9h11
Effluents from various industries such as tannery,
paper, sulfur-dyeing unit, etc. contain considerable
amounts of di†erent sulfur compounds, mainly various
metallic sulÐdes, which at a concentration level of
100 mg dm~3 a†ect the microbial treatment of these
wastewaters. Moreover, the discharge of these effluents
containing sulÐdes above 2 mg dm~3 is not permissible
due to the detrimental e†ect arising from the evolution
of malodorous hydrogen sulÐde. Hence, the stringent
environment protection laws have made the removal of
sulÐdes from industrial wastewaters a prime target.
Among the various methods available for oxidation of
sulÐde-bearing wastewaters, the air oxidation process
using
catalysts
such
as
activated
carbon,12
metal
oxides,13,14 surfactant,15 etc. is well known. However, it
has been found that the catalytic air oxidation process
alone cannot reduce the sulÐde concentration of the
effluents up to the speciÐed discharge limit and the
separation problem of the catalyst such as activated
carbon from the treated effluent leads to the escalation
of the process cost.
Hydrogen peroxide is an eco-friendly, strong oxidant
and effluents treated with it are more susceptible to bio-
degradation.16,17 Hence, hydrogen peroxide in the pres-
ence of a catalyst is gaining importance for secondary
or even primary treatment of industrial effluents, includ-
ing sulÐde-bearing wastewaters, to meet the desired dis-
charge limit. In this paper, we have reported the
potential of coal Ñy ash as a heterogeneous catalyst for
decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in sodium hydrox-
ide solutions and oxidation of aqueous sodium sulÐde
solutions selected as a model sulÐde-bearing effluent,
with hydrogen peroxide.
2
EXPERIMENTAL
All experiments were carried out batchwise in a mecha-
nically agitated glass reactor with an internal diameter
of 9É5 ] 10~2 m. A six-bladed disc-turbine impeller
made of glass (3É0 ] 10~2 m in diameter) was used for
agitation. The reactor was immersed in a water bath
whose temperature can be controlled within ^1¡C.

Hydrogen peroxide is an eco-friendly, strong oxidant
and effluents treated with it are more susceptible to bio-
degradation.16,17 Hence, hydrogen peroxide in the pres-
ence of a catalyst is gaining importance for secondary
or even primary treatment of industrial effluents, includ-
ing sulÐde-bearing wastewaters, to meet the desired dis-
charge limit. In this paper, we have reported the
potential of coal Ñy ash as a heterogeneous catalyst for
decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in sodium hydrox-
ide solutions and oxidation of aqueous sodium sulÐde
solutions selected as a model sulÐde-bearing effluent,
All experiments were carried out batchwise in a mecha-
nically agitated glass reactor with an internal diameter
of 9É5 ] 10~2 m. A six-bladed disc-turbine impeller
made of glass (3É0 ] 10~2 m in diameter) was used for
agitation. The reactor was immersed in a water bath
whose temperature can be controlled within ^1¡C.
Use of coal Ñy ash as a heterogeneous catalyst
In each run, 2É5 ] 10~4 m3 aqueous solution of
sodium sulÐde of known concentration was charged
into the reactor and was heated up to the preÐxed tem-
perature. To check the e†ect of aerial oxidation of
sulÐde, some experiments were carried out in an inert
atmosphere of nitrogen. But it was found that the rates
of oxidation of sodium sulÐde in the presence as well as
in the absence of the inert atmosphere remained the
same. Before the addition of the required quantity of
hydrogen peroxide (30% (w/v)) and Ñy ash, the exact
concentration of sodium sulÐde in the heated solution
was determined. Just after the addition of hydrogen per-
oxide and Ñy ash, the reaction mixture was agitated at a
particular speed. Samples were withdrawn at regular
intervals of time from the reaction mixture to estimate
unreacted sodium sulÐde after stopping the agitation for
a few seconds to allow the Ñy ash particles to settle
down. Since the presence of hydrogen peroxide in the
reaction mixture interfered with the estimation of
sodium sulÐde by the standard iodometric method, its
conversion was determined by analyzing the unreacted
sodium sulÐde using a complexometric titration method
as reported by Roy and De.18 This method was based
on the quantitative precipitation of sulÐde as insoluble
cadmium sulÐde by adding excess of a known amount
of soluble bivalent salt of cadmium to the solution. The
remaining excess of cadmium ion in the solution was
then estimated by titrating a Ðxed volume of the super-
natant liquid against standardized EDTA solution at
pH 10 using Erichrome black T as indicator. As the rate
of oxidation of sodium sulÐde was reported to be
dependent on the presence of metallic impurities,12,19
all experiments were carried out using sodium sulÐde
from a single stock. Technical grade sodium sulÐde
(fused Ñakes) was obtained from S.D. Fine-Chem. Pvt.
Ltd, Bombay, India. Fly ashes were procured from four
Bengal, India and denoted by FA1, FA2, etc. The physi-
cal properties and bulk chemical composition of these
Ñy ashes were determined and are given in Table 1.
The leachability of Fe, Mn, Cu and K from FA1 Ñy
ash in di†erent leaching media were tested at 303 K and
323 K, using the same batch reactor as mentioned
above. The concentrations of these elements in the
leachates were determined by an Atomic Absorption
Spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Model 380). All
leaching experiments with NaOH and
under di†erent operating conditions were repeated at
least twice and in each case the deviation was found to
The nature of the intermediate and product for the oxi-
dation of sulÐdes in aqueous solution strongly depend
on the pH of reaction medium as well as on the nature
of the oxidant. Chen and Morris20 reported polysulÐde,
as an intermediate in neutral and weakly
alkaline media for oxidation of sulÐdes with oxygen,
whereas, Lefers et al.21 reported that at pH [ 9 the for-
mation of polysulÐde is very unlikely. In the case of air
oxidation of sodium sulÐde, Avrahami and Golding22
proposed that at pH [ 11 thiosulfate,
stable product and its oxidation to sulfate,
In this study, a qualitative analysis of the reaction
mixture (pH [ 12É5) was done to identify the product(s)
of hydrogen peroxide oxidation of sodium sulÐde in the
presence of 4% (w/v) FA1 ash loading. It was found
that at 303 K, for an initial sodium sulÐde and hydro-
gen peroxide concentrations of 26É98 ] 10~2 kmol m~3
and 24É28 ] 10~2 kmol m~3 respectively, after 1 h
reaction the mixture contained thiosulfate. However,
even after 2 h reaction it was found that the reaction
mixture contained primarily thiosulfate and traces of
sulfate, i.e. the oxidation of sodium sulÐde in the pres-
ence of hydrogen peroxide primarily gives thiosulfate
whose oxidation to sulfate is very slow. So, the probable

Decomposition of hydrogen peroxide with and
without Ñy ash in alkaline medium
The presence of sulÐde ions in the reaction mixture
interfered with the estimation of undecomposed hydro-
gen peroxide by the standard iodometric method in the
course of the reaction and according to our knowledge
there is no information in the literature on the kinetics
of decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the presence
of Ñy ash in an alkaline medium. Hence, the kinetics of
decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the presence of
Ñy ash were studied separately in aqueous solutions of
sodium hydroxide in the pH range of 12É5È13É5, which
was normally encountered in aqueous solutions of

The leaching characteristics of Ñy ash were studied to
Ðnd out the catalytic e†ect of any leached metal ion
such as Fe, Cu, Mn, etc. from Ñy ash matrix, on the
decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in sodium hydrox-
The e†ects of Ñy ash loading, speed of agitation,
initial concentrations of hydrogen peroxide and sodium
hydroxide, electrolyte and temperature on the decom-
position of hydrogen peroxide were studied.
The leachability of Fe, Cu and Mn, which generally
catalyze the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide, was
studied at 303 K and 323 K in 0É25 M sodium hydrox-
ide solution for 4% (w/v) loading of FA1 ash. It was
found that in the course of leaching, up to 1 h, the con-
centrations of all these metal ions in the leachates were
below their detection limit. Hence, one may assume that
the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the presence
of Ñy ash is all due to the heterogeneous catalytic e†ect
of Ñy ash. Yet, to conÐrm it the decomposition of
hydrogen peroxide was also studied in the leachates at
323 K. It was found that the extent of decomposition
was same as in 0É25 M sodium hydroxide solution
without Ñy ash under otherwise identical operating con-
ditions. Hence, we may conclude that the decomposi-
Since Ñy ash particles are almost non-porous in nature
(Table 1), a homogeneous reaction model can be used to
analyze the experimental data.23 A Ðrst order irrevers-
ible reaction model was assumed for the decomposition
of hydrogen peroxide and all experimental data were
analyzed by integral method of analysis. Figure 1 shows
the e†ect of FA1 ash loading in the range of 1È6% (w/v)
on the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide for its
initial concentration of 24É28 ] 10~2 kmol m~3 at
303 K. The experimental data were found to correlate
well with the proposed Ðrst order reaction model (Fig.
1). The values of speciÐc Ðrst order rate constants, k@
(s~1), were calculated from the slopes of the above plots.
It was found that the value of k@ increased from
2É067 ] 10~4 s~1 to 13É341 ] 10~4 s~1 as the ash
loading was increased from 1% (w/v) to 6% (w/v) under
otherwise identical operating conditions. The higher
increasing ash loading was due to the availability of
higher speciÐc surface area of catalyst, i.e. of Ñy ash.
It was found that the speed of agitation in the range of
500È1500 rev min~1 had negligible e†ect on the decom-

presence of 4% (w/v) FA1 ash loading. It was found
that at 303 K for an initial hydrogen peroxide concen-
tration of 25É06 ] 10~2 kmol m~3, on increasing the

8É5%, whereas at 323 K the value of k@ increased by
20% under otherwise identical operating conditions. So,
one may therefore conclude that the external mass
transfer resistance has almost negligible e†ect on the
decomposition of hydrogen peroxide even at 323 K.

lent to 50 mg dm~3 Fe2` ion, led to the immediate

detection limit. Another experiment was also performed

rate of oxidation of sodium sulÐde was found to be

The absence of soluble iron in the Ðltrate of the reaction
mixture conÐrmed that the oxidation occurred only on
the surface of the catalyst. So, these studies have con-
Ðrmed that the enhancement of the rate of oxidation of
sodium sulÐde with hydrogen peroxide on addition of

The e†ect of speed of agitation was studied in the range
of 500È1500 rev min~1 on the oxidation of aqueous
sodium sulÐde solutions in the presence of 4% (w/v)
FA1 ash loading. The initial concentrations of sodium
sulÐde and hydrogen peroxide were 26É98 ] 10~2 kmol
m~3 and 24É28 ^ 0É2 ] 10~2 kmol m~3 respectively. It
was observed that although the speed of agitation had a
nominal e†ect below 800 rev min~1 on the rate of oxi-
dation of sodium sulÐde at 303 K, it had no e†ect above
800 rev min~1. So, external mass transfer resistance had
no e†ect on the rate of the reaction above 800 rev
min~1. The low porosity of Ñy ash particles (Table 1),
the negligible e†ect of external mass transfer resistance
and the e†ect of catalyst loading suggested a surface-
controlled reaction for this system.

The deactivation of catalytic activity of FA1 ash was
studied at 303 K. After each batch (batch time equal to
7200 s) Ñy ash was separated from the reaction mixture
by Ðltration, dried and cooled to 303 K. The weight loss
of Ñy ash after each batch was found to be less than
2É5% and before going to the next batch, the required
quantity of fresh FA1 ash was added to the reused ash
to make up the loss and thus 4% (w/v) ash loading was
maintained for each batch. It can be seen from Fig. 10
that after the Ðrst repeated use the rate of oxidation

Use of coal Ñy ash as a heterogeneous catalyst

4% (w/v) FA1 ash; N: 1000 rev min~1.
remained almost constant even after six repeated uses.
About 2È3% (w/w) of total mass of Ñy ash is water
soluble and this fraction contains metal ions such as
Na`, K`, Ca2`, Mg2`, Fe2`, etc.1 However, the leach-
ability of these metal ions primarily depends upon the
nature of the Ñy ash and pH of the leaching medium.24
All the Ñy ashes used in this work were alkaline in
nature (Table 1). It was found that for 4% (w/v) loading
of FA1 ash at 303 K and 323 K, the concentrations of
Fe, Cu and Mn in distilled water extract (pH 8É0 ^ 0É1)
were below their detection limit. But the distilled water
Fig. 11. X-ray di†ractographs of fresh and six repeated use
extracts contained Na` and K` in the range of 15È
20 mg dm~3 and 25È30 mg dm~3 respectively. This
result suggested that leached Fe2`, Cu2`, Mn2`, etc.
are instantaneously precipitated at pH º 8 as their
insoluble hydroxides and thereby did not a†ect the
decomposition of hydrogen peroxide as well as the oxi-
dation of sodium sulÐde. However, the leaching of these
metal ions deactivates the catalyst surface and thereby
the rate of oxidation was about 20% higher for fresh
FA1 ash than that for reused FA1 ash of Ðrst cycle.
After the Ðrst repeated use the rates of oxidation
remained almost unchanged even after six repeated
uses. The X-ray di†ractographs (Fig. 11) also show that
the intensity of peaks of crystalline phases of the six-
time repeated use FA1 ash is slightly lower than that of
fresh FA1 ash. It can also be seen from Fig. 11 that the
rutile phase (peak no. 11) is absent in the reused FA1
ash and this is quite expected due to the dissolution of
rutile by the action of hydrogen peroxide in course of
the reaction. Therefore, the dissolution of the rutile
phase, as well as leaching of some other phases from the
matrix of FA1 ash, deactivates the surface of the ash
particles for its catalytic e†ect.
In this work, the potential of Ñy ash procured from
coal-Ðred thermal power plants has been proved as a
heterogeneous catalyst in aqueous phase decomposition
of hydrogen peroxide and in oxidation of sodium sulÐde
solutions using hydrogen peroxide. The decomposition
of hydrogen peroxide in the presence of FA1 ash fol-
lowed a Ðrst order reaction with respect to its concen-
12É56 ] 10~2È41É15 ] 10~2 kmol m~3. At 303 K, 4%
(w/v) FA1 ash loading enhanced the rate of decomposi-
tion of hydrogen peroxide by a factor of 19É96. The
apparent activation energy for decomposition of hydro-
gen peroxide using 4% (w/v) FA1 ash loading was
found to be 9É38 kcal gmol~1 and the corresponding
value without FA1 ash was 18É72 kcal gmol~1.
Only 4% (w/v) Ñy ash loading intensiÐed the rate of
oxidation of sodium sulÐde with hydrogen peroxide by
a factor of 4É52 over that without Ñy ash at 303 K. In
the presence of Ñy ash, temperature in the range of 303È
323 K had negligible e†ect on the oxidation of sodium
sulÐde with hydrogen peroxide and at 303 K, 4% (w/v)
FA1 ash loading was found to be the optimum catalyst
loading for this reaction. The presence of electrolytes in
the reaction medium decreased the rate of oxidation of
sodium sulÐde and deactivation of the catalytic e†ect
was found to be less than 20% even after six repeated
Hence, oxidation of sulÐde-bearing effluents with
hydrogen peroxide in the presence of Ñy ash as catalyst
seems to be quite an economically attractive treatment
D. Mallick, S. Khanra, S. K. Chaudhuri
process for the following reasons: very low cost of Ñy
ash, less than 20% deactivation even after a number of
repeated uses, and easy separation from the treated
